parents.father_research_notes: Stephen Root, son of Timothy and Mary (Langdon) Root of Farmington, Connecticut. Parents non-Hooker — not built. Source: Edward Hooker 1909 entry #445.

death.cause: Died in infancy 1795 per Edward Hooker entry #1151.

parents.father_research_notes: Lyman Brooks Cowles. Sam Hooker family records trace descent as: Samuel Cowles Jr (great-grandfather) → Joseph Cowles (b. 1747, grandfather) → Joseph Brooks Cowles (b. 1774, father) → Lyman Brooks Cowles (b. 1795). Samuel Cowles Jr's identification and any Hooker-line connection is PENDING — not currently traceable in this database. The Cowles family of Farmington CT has multiple Hooker intermarriages so a Hooker-line connection is plausible but unverified.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 (referenced); ancestry data from family records

career.notes: EH 1909 #1155 records 'farmer at Farmington, Conn.' — likely an EH error or referring to an earlier residence; FamilySearch evidence places Timothy and family in Ashtabula Ohio from at least the 1841 marriage onward.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at sixty-three; only daughter at sixty-six
narrative_blocks.body: William Ezekiel Root's first marriage to Harriet Leonard around 1827 produced five sons. After their separation or her departure (she 'still living' at his 1889 death and later married Orlin W. Spencer), William remarried on July 23, 1869 in Lenox Township Ohio to Paulina Monroe — and three years later, at age sixty-six, fathered his only documented daughter Mary E. Root. The late-life second-family arc was uncommon among Connecticut-Hooker-line men of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Catskill NY birth, EH misrecorded as Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: William Ezekiel Root was born in 1806 in Catskill New York, where his parents had settled before 1792 — though some early genealogical records mistakenly placed his birth in Farmington Connecticut. He died in Lenox Township, Ashtabula County Ohio on August 31, 1889 at age eighty-three, buried at West Lenox Cemetery. The Connecticut-to-New-York-to-Ohio migration arc spanned three states across his lifetime.

narrative_blocks.header: Five sons, four documented only by initials
narrative_blocks.body: William and Harriet's five sons were William Alonzo Root born 1832, and four others known in records only as E.L. (1859), E.W. (1860), S.L. (1860), and A.A. (1861). The use of initials for four of five sons may reflect contemporary practice or simply that fuller given names were never preserved. The 1859-1861 birth cluster of four boys came in close succession during William's middle-aged years.

parents.father_research_notes: Paulina Monroe. m1 Edwin Sikes (one child by that marriage) — both non-Hooker, not built. After Edwin's death m2 William Ezekiel Root.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children with Margaret Catharine Fern
narrative_blocks.body: William Alonzo Root married Margaret Catharine Fern about 1882; she was born August 1852 in New York. Their three documented children were Edgar Francis Root (1883-1941, born Buffalo New York and died Bluefield West Virginia), Ethel M. Root (born 1885), and Margaret E. Root. Edgar Francis's career took him from Buffalo to West Virginia — a substantial geographic move from the family's Ashtabula County Ohio center.

narrative_blocks.header: Ohio birth, Ontario death; eldest of five
narrative_blocks.body: William Alonzo Root was the eldest of his father William Ezekiel Root's five sons born in Austinburg, Ashtabula County Ohio in the late 1850s and early 1860s. The five brothers — William Alonzo, E.L., E.W., S.L., and A.A. — formed an unusually compressed 1859-1861 birth cluster. William Alonzo eventually moved north into Ontario Canada, dying in Wentworth at age about seventy-one in 1930.

sources.short: Genealogy of the Cowles family in America (Cowles, 1929)
Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles

career.title: Selectman of Farmington
career.notes: Documented as Selectman of Farmington on January 10, 1816 when he co-signed the certificate of examination for the manumission of Titus, slave of Capt. Joshua Youngs (alongside Justice of the Peace John Mix and fellow Selectman Samuel Richards). Per Trumbull's Memorial History of Hartford County (1886), Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles.

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Abigail married her husband's younger brother James.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Cowles 1639-1691 of Farmington CT (not built). Per WikiTree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Stanley Cowles 1637-1734 of Farmington CT (not built). Per WikiTree.

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Andrews sisters in succession
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Mary Andrews died July 1705; four years later in November 1709 he married her sister Sarah Andrews. Sarah herself died June 1715 leaving only one infant who had died in 1714 — Isaac married for the third and last time in December 1716 to Elizabeth Smith, twenty-six years his junior.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington tailor who became Captain by 1731
narrative_blocks.body: A tailor by trade and extensive landholder at Farmington, he rose through the militia ranks — Ensign 1717, Lieutenant 1723, Captain by May 1731 of the 2nd Company Trainband. He also served as Company Clerk in 1714, collector of the minister's rate 1698-1700, constable for several years, and town representative in 1726.

His will was dated October 1740 and proved March 1756. Seven years later in 1763 his last five surviving sons divided his Farmington estate — approximately £2,500 among themselves as sole heirs, an unusually concentrated inheritance for the colonial period.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: His death at 96 killed his brother Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: The well stone on Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: He and Martha lived on the George Cowles place on Main Street, Farmington. In Edward Hooker's time (1909), a flat stone in the sidewalk just below the brow of the hill still covered the old well of the Giles Hooker house. Directly opposite, his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had lived in the Mix House — just south of the old burying ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather was Secretary of State
narrative_blocks.body: Her maternal grandfather was Hon. John Allyn (Allen), Secretary of State for the Colony of Connecticut. Her father, Capt. Aaron Cooke IV, served as Hartford's deputy to the Connecticut General Assembly for fifteen sessions and chaired the committee to distribute the new laws in 1702.
